mn12:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Vesālī, outside the city, in a stretch of woodland in the outlying district.
mn12:10.1: Here, Sāriputta, the Tathāgata understands as it truly is what is possible as possible, and what is impossible as impossible.
mn12:10.2: And that the Tathāgata understands as it truly is what is possible as possible and what is impossible as impossible—this too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:11.1: Again, Sāriputta, the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the ripening of undertakings of *kamma*, past, future, and present, according to grounds and causes.
mn12:11.2: And that the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the ripening of undertakings of *kamma*, past, future, and present, according to grounds and causes—this too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:12.1: Again, Sāriputta, the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the way leading to every destination.
mn12:12.2: And that the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the way leading to every destination—this too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:13.1: Again, Sāriputta, the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the world with its many and various elements.
mn12:13.2: And that the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the world with its many and various elements—this too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:14.1: Again, Sāriputta, the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the various inclinations of beings.
mn12:14.2: And that the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the various inclinations of beings—this too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:15.1: Again, Sāriputta, the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the higher and lower *indriyas* of other beings, other persons.
mn12:15.2: And that the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the higher and lower *indriyas* of other beings, other persons—this too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:16.1: Again, Sāriputta, the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the defilement, purification, and emergence regarding *jhānas*, liberations, *samādhis*, and attainments.
mn12:16.2: And that the Tathāgata understands as it truly is the defilement, purification, and emergence regarding *jhānas*, liberations, *samādhis*, and attainments—this too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:17.1: Again, Sāriputta, the Tathāgata recollects his manifold past lives, that is: one birth, two births, three births, four births, five births, ten births, twenty births, thirty births, forty births, fifty births, a hundred births, a thousand births, a hundred thousand births; many eons of contraction, many eons of expansion, many eons of contraction and expansion: ‘There I was so named, of such a clan, of such an appearance, with such food, experiencing such *sukha* and *dukkha*, with such a lifespan. Passing away from there, I arose elsewhere; there too I was so named, of such a clan, of such an appearance, with such food, experiencing such *sukha* and *dukkha*, with such a lifespan. Passing away from there, I reappeared here.’ Thus he recollects his manifold past lives in their aspects and details.
mn12:17.2: And that the Tathāgata recollects his manifold past lives, that is: one birth, two births … thus he recollects his manifold past lives in their aspects and details—this too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:18.1: Again, Sāriputta, the Tathāgata, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, sees beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, fortunate and unfortunate, and understands beings as faring according to their *kamma*: ‘These beings, indeed, were endowed with bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct; revilers of the noble ones, holding wrong view, undertaking *kamma* based on wrong view. At the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a state of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell. But these beings were endowed with bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct; not revilers of the noble ones, holding right view, undertaking *kamma* based on right view. At the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a good destination, a heavenly world.’ Thus, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, he sees beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, fortunate and unfortunate, and understands beings as faring according to their *kamma*.
mn12:18.2: And that the Tathāgata, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, sees beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, fortunate and unfortunate, and understands beings as faring according to their *kamma*: ‘These beings, indeed, were endowed with bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct; revilers of the noble ones, holding wrong view, undertaking *kamma* based on wrong view. At the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a state of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell. But these beings were endowed with bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct; not revilers of the noble ones, holding right view, undertaking *kamma* based on right view. At the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a good destination, a heavenly world.’ Thus, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, he sees beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, fortunate and unfortunate, and understands beings as faring according to their *kamma*. This too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:19.1: Again, Sāriputta, through the destruction of the *āsavas*, the Tathāgata dwells having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the taintless liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*].
mn12:19.2: And that through the destruction of the *āsavas*, the Tathāgata dwells having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the taintless liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*]—this too is a Tathāgata *bala*, on account of which the Tathāgata claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:2.1: Now at that time Sunakkhatta, the Licchavi’s son, had recently left this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayā*].
mn12:2.2: In an assembly at Vesālī he spoke thus:
mn12:2.3: “The ascetic Gotama has no higher-than-human state, no distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.
mn12:2.4: The ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* hammered out by reasoning, following inquiry, of his own inspiration.
mn12:2.5: And for whose sake that *dhamma* is taught, it leads one who acts in accordance with it to the complete ending of *dukkha*.”
mn12:20.1: These, Sāriputta, are the ten Tathāgata *balas* by which the Tathāgata, endowed with those *balas*, claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:21.1: Whoever, Sāriputta, would say of me, knowing and seeing thus:
mn12:21.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama has no higher-than-human state, no distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones;
mn12:21.3: the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* hammered out by reasoning, following inquiry, of his own inspiration,’
mn12:21.4: then, Sāriputta, unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:21.5: Just as, Sāriputta, a *bhikkhu* accomplished in ethical conduct, accomplished in *samādhi*, accomplished in discernment [*paññā*], could realize final knowledge in this very life, so certain is this state of affairs, Sāriputta, I say.
mn12:21.6: Unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:22.1: There are these four grounds of self-confidence of the Tathāgata, Sāriputta, by which the Tathāgata, endowed with those grounds of self-confidence, claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:23.1: ‘While you claim perfect awakening, these things are not fully awakened by you.’ I do not see any basis, Sāriputta, on account of which any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or Brahmā or anyone at all in the world could challenge me on this in accordance with *dhamma*.
mn12:23.2: Seeing no such basis, Sāriputta, I dwell having attained security, attained fearlessness, attained self-confidence.
mn12:24.1: ‘While you claim that your *āsavas* are destroyed, these *āsavas* are not entirely destroyed.’ I do not see any basis, Sāriputta, on account of which any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or Brahmā or anyone at all in the world could challenge me on this in accordance with *dhamma*.
mn12:24.2: Seeing no such basis, Sāriputta, I dwell having attained security, attained fearlessness, attained self-confidence.
mn12:25.1: ‘Those obstructive *dhammas* that you have spoken of are not really capable of obstructing one who engages in them.’ I do not see any basis, Sāriputta, on account of which any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or Brahmā or anyone at all in the world could challenge me on this in accordance with *dhamma*.
mn12:25.2: Seeing no such basis, Sāriputta, I dwell having attained security, attained fearlessness, attained self-confidence.
mn12:26.1: ‘The *dhamma* you have taught for that purpose does not lead one who acts in accordance with it to the complete ending of *dukkha*.’ I do not see any basis, Sāriputta, on account of which any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or Brahmā or anyone at all in the world could challenge me on this in accordance with *dhamma*.
mn12:26.2: Seeing no such basis, Sāriputta, I dwell having attained security, attained fearlessness, attained self-confidence.
mn12:27.1: These, Sāriputta, are the four grounds of self-confidence of the Tathāgata by which the Tathāgata, endowed with those grounds of self-confidence, claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
mn12:28.1: Whoever, Sāriputta, would say of me, knowing and seeing thus:
mn12:28.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama has no higher-than-human state, no distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones; the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* hammered out by reasoning, following inquiry, of his own inspiration,’
mn12:28.3: then, Sāriputta, unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:28.4: Just as, Sāriputta, a *bhikkhu* accomplished in ethical conduct, accomplished in *samādhi*, accomplished in discernment [*paññā*], could realize final knowledge in this very life, so certain is this state of affairs, Sāriputta, I say.
mn12:28.5: Unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:29.1: There are these eight assemblies, Sāriputta.
mn12:29.3: The assembly of nobles, the assembly of brahmins, the assembly of householders, the assembly of ascetics, the assembly of the Four Great Kings, the assembly of the Thirty-Three, the assembly of Māra, the assembly of Brahmā—
mn12:29.4: these are the eight assemblies, Sāriputta.
mn12:29.5: Endowed with these four grounds of self-confidence, the Tathāgata approaches and enters into these eight assemblies.
mn12:29.6: I recall, Sāriputta, having approached many hundreds of assemblies of nobles.
mn12:29.7: There too I have sat before, spoken before, and engaged in discussion before.
mn12:29.8: And I do not see any basis, Sāriputta, on account of which fear or timidity might come over me there.
mn12:29.9: Seeing no such basis, Sāriputta, I dwell having attained security, attained fearlessness, attained self-confidence.
mn12:3.1: Then in the morning Venerable Sāriputta dressed, took his bowl and robe, and entered Vesālī for alms.
mn12:3.10: The ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* hammered out by reasoning, following inquiry, of his own inspiration.
mn12:3.11: And for whose sake that *dhamma* is taught, it leads one who acts in accordance with it to the complete ending of *dukkha*.’”
mn12:3.2: Venerable Sāriputta heard Sunakkhatta, the Licchavi’s son, speaking thus in an assembly at Vesālī:
mn12:3.3: “The ascetic Gotama has no higher-than-human state, no distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.
mn12:3.4: The ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* hammered out by reasoning, following inquiry, of his own inspiration.
mn12:3.5: And for whose sake that *dhamma* is taught, it leads one who acts in accordance with it to the complete ending of *dukkha*.”
mn12:3.6: Then, after wandering for alms in Vesālī and returning from his almsround after the meal, Venerable Sāriputta went to the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, Venerable Sāriputta said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn12:3.7: “Sunakkhatta, *bhante*, the Licchavi’s son, has recently left this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayā*].
mn12:3.8: In an assembly at Vesālī he speaks thus:
mn12:3.9: ‘The ascetic Gotama has no higher-than-human state, no distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.
mn12:30.1: I recall, Sāriputta, having approached many hundreds of assemblies of brahmins …
mn12:30.10: Seeing no such basis, Sāriputta, I dwell having attained security, attained fearlessness, attained self-confidence.
mn12:30.4: assemblies of the Four Great Kings …
mn12:30.8: There too I have sat before, spoken before, and engaged in discussion before.
mn12:30.9: And I do not see any basis, Sāriputta, on account of which fear or timidity might come over me there.
mn12:31.1: Whoever, Sāriputta, would say of me, knowing and seeing thus:
mn12:31.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama has no higher-than-human state, no distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones; the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* hammered out by reasoning, following inquiry, of his own inspiration,’
mn12:31.3: then, Sāriputta, unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:31.4: Just as, Sāriputta, a *bhikkhu* accomplished in ethical conduct, accomplished in *samādhi*, accomplished in discernment [*paññā*], could realize final knowledge in this very life, so certain is this state of affairs, Sāriputta, I say.
mn12:31.5: Unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:32.1: There are these four modes of birth, Sāriputta.
mn12:32.3: Birth from an egg, birth from a womb, birth from moisture, and spontaneous birth.
mn12:33.1: And what, Sāriputta, is birth from an egg?
mn12:33.10: And what, Sāriputta, is spontaneous birth?
mn12:33.11: Gods, hell-beings, some humans, and some beings in states of loss—
mn12:33.12: this is called spontaneous birth, Sāriputta.
mn12:33.13: These, Sāriputta, are the four modes of birth.
mn12:33.2: Those beings who are born by breaking out of an eggshell—
mn12:33.3: this is called birth from an egg, Sāriputta.
mn12:33.4: And what, Sāriputta, is birth from a womb?
mn12:33.5: Those beings who are born by breaking out of a womb-sac—
mn12:33.6: this is called birth from a womb, Sāriputta.
mn12:33.7: And what, Sāriputta, is birth from moisture?
mn12:33.8: Those beings who are born in rotting fish, or in a rotting corpse, or in sour porridge, or in cesspools, or in filthy drains—
mn12:33.9: this is called birth from moisture, Sāriputta.
mn12:34.1: Whoever, Sāriputta, would say of me, knowing and seeing thus:
mn12:34.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama has no higher-than-human state, no distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones; the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* hammered out by reasoning, following inquiry, of his own inspiration,’
mn12:34.3: then, Sāriputta, unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:34.4: Just as, Sāriputta, a *bhikkhu* accomplished in ethical conduct, accomplished in *samādhi*, accomplished in discernment [*paññā*], could realize final knowledge in this very life, so certain is this state of affairs, Sāriputta, I say.
mn12:34.5: Unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:35.1: There are these five destinations, Sāriputta.
mn12:35.3: Hell, the animal womb, the realm of ghosts, humans, and gods.
mn12:36.1: And I understand hell, Sāriputta, and the path going to hell, and the way of practice going to hell;
mn12:36.10: and I understand that mode of practice by which, at the breakup of the body, after death, one arises in a good destination, a heavenly world.
mn12:36.11: And I understand *nibbāna*, Sāriputta, and the path going to *nibbāna*, and the way of practice going to *nibbāna*;
mn12:36.12: and I understand that mode of practice by which, through the destruction of the *āsavas*, one dwells having realized for oneself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the taintless liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*].
mn12:36.2: and I understand that mode of practice by which, at the breakup of the body, after death, one arises in a state of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell.
mn12:36.3: And I understand the animal womb, Sāriputta, and the path going to the animal womb, and the way of practice going to the animal womb;
mn12:36.4: and I understand that mode of practice by which, at the breakup of the body, after death, one arises in the animal womb.
mn12:36.5: And I understand the realm of ghosts, Sāriputta, and the path going to the realm of ghosts, and the way of practice going to the realm of ghosts;
mn12:36.6: and I understand that mode of practice by which, at the breakup of the body, after death, one arises in the realm of ghosts.
mn12:36.7: And I understand humans, Sāriputta, and the path going to the human world, and the way of practice going to the human world;
mn12:36.8: and I understand that mode of practice by which, at the breakup of the body, after death, one arises among humans.
mn12:36.9: And I understand gods, Sāriputta, and the path going to the world of gods, and the way of practice going to the world of gods;
mn12:37.1: Here, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:37.10: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise in a state of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell.’
mn12:37.11: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen in a state of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell, experiencing wholly painful, intense, bitter *vedanā*s.
mn12:37.2: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and having undertaken this path, at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise in a state of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell.’
mn12:37.3: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen in a state of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell, experiencing wholly painful, intense, bitter *vedanā*s.
mn12:37.4: Suppose, Sāriputta, there were a pit of glowing coals deeper than a man’s height, full of coals, without flame and without smoke.
mn12:37.5: Then a man might come, scorched by the heat, oppressed by the heat, weary, thirsty, parched, heading along a single road straight toward that very pit of coals.
mn12:37.6: A man with good eyesight, seeing him, might say:
mn12:37.7: ‘This worthy man is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that he will come to this very pit of coals.’
mn12:37.8: Then later he might see him fallen into that pit of coals, experiencing wholly painful, intense, bitter *vedanā*s.
mn12:37.9: In the same way, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person here by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:38.1: Here again, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:38.10: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise in the animal womb.’
mn12:38.11: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen in the animal womb, experiencing painful, intense, bitter *vedanā*s.
mn12:38.2: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise in the animal womb.’
mn12:38.3: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen in the animal womb, experiencing painful, intense, bitter *vedanā*s.
mn12:38.4: Suppose, Sāriputta, there were a cesspit deeper than a man’s height, full of excrement.
mn12:38.5: Then a man might come, scorched by the heat, oppressed by the heat, weary, thirsty, parched, heading along a single road straight toward that very cesspit.
mn12:38.6: A man with good eyesight, seeing him, might say:
mn12:38.7: ‘This worthy man is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that he will come to this very cesspit.’
mn12:38.8: Then later he might see him fallen into that cesspit, experiencing painful, intense, bitter *vedanā*s.
mn12:38.9: In the same way, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person here by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:39.1: Here again, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:39.10: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise in the realm of ghosts.’
mn12:39.11: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen in the realm of ghosts, experiencing *vedanā*s with much *dukkha*.
mn12:39.2: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise in the realm of ghosts.’
mn12:39.3: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen in the realm of ghosts, experiencing *vedanā*s with much *dukkha*.
mn12:39.4: Suppose, Sāriputta, there were a tree grown on uneven ground, with sparse leaves and scant shade.
mn12:39.5: Then a man might come, scorched by the heat, oppressed by the heat, weary, thirsty, parched, heading along a single road straight toward that very tree.
mn12:39.6: A man with good eyesight, seeing him, might say:
mn12:39.7: ‘This worthy man is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that he will come to this very tree.’
mn12:39.8: Then later he might see him sitting or lying in the shade of that tree, experiencing *vedanā*s with much *dukkha*.
mn12:39.9: In the same way, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person here by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:4.1: “He is angry, Sāriputta, that Sunakkhatta, that foolish man.
mn12:4.2: And it is out of anger that he has spoken those words.
mn12:4.3: Thinking, ‘I will speak dispraise,’ Sunakkhatta, that foolish man, speaks only praise of the Tathāgata.
mn12:4.4: For it is praise of the Tathāgata, Sāriputta, if one were to say:
mn12:4.5: ‘And for whose sake that *dhamma* is taught, it leads one who acts in accordance with it to the complete ending of *dukkha*.’
mn12:40.1: Here again, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:40.10: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise among humans.’
mn12:40.11: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen among humans, experiencing *vedanā*s with much *sukha*.
mn12:40.2: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise among humans.’
mn12:40.3: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen among humans, experiencing *vedanā*s with much *sukha*.
mn12:40.4: Suppose, Sāriputta, there were a tree grown on level ground, with thick leaves and dense shade.
mn12:40.5: Then a man might come, scorched by the heat, oppressed by the heat, weary, thirsty, parched, heading along a single road straight toward that very tree.
mn12:40.6: A man with good eyesight, seeing him, might say:
mn12:40.7: ‘This worthy man is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that he will come to this very tree.’
mn12:40.8: Then later he might see him sitting or lying in the shade of that tree, experiencing *vedanā*s with much *sukha*.
mn12:40.9: In the same way, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person here by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:41.1: Here again, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*]:
mn12:41.10: In the same way, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person here by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:41.11: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise in a good destination, a heavenly world.’
mn12:41.12: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen in a good destination, a heavenly world, experiencing wholly pleasant *vedanā*s.
mn12:41.2: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that at the breakup of the body, after death, he will arise in a good destination, a heavenly world.’
mn12:41.3: Then later I see him, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, after the breakup of the body, after death, arisen in a good destination, a heavenly world, experiencing wholly pleasant *vedanā*s.
mn12:41.4: Suppose, Sāriputta, there were a mansion, and in it an upper chamber, plastered inside and out, draft-free, with well-fitted door-bolts and shuttered windows.
mn12:41.5: And there would be in it a couch spread with woollen coverlets, white coverlets, embroidered coverlets, and a fine deer-hide spread, with a canopy above and red cushions at both ends.
mn12:41.6: Then a man might come, scorched by the heat, oppressed by the heat, weary, thirsty, parched, heading along a single road straight toward that very mansion.
mn12:41.7: A man with good eyesight, seeing him, might say:
mn12:41.8: ‘This worthy man is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that he will come to this very mansion.’
mn12:41.9: Then later he might see him sitting or lying in that mansion, in that upper chamber, on that couch, experiencing wholly pleasant *vedanā*s.
mn12:42.1: Here again, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:42.10: In the same way, Sāriputta, I understand a certain person here by encompassing his mind [*ceto*] with my mind [*cetasā*] thus:
mn12:42.11: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that through the destruction of the *āsavas*, he will dwell having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the taintless liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*].’
mn12:42.12: Then later I see him dwelling, through the destruction of the *āsavas*, having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the taintless liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], experiencing wholly pleasant *vedanā*s.
mn12:42.13: These, Sāriputta, are the five destinations.
mn12:42.2: ‘This person is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that through the destruction of the *āsavas*, he will dwell having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the taintless liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*].’
mn12:42.3: Then later I see him dwelling, through the destruction of the *āsavas*, having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the taintless liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], experiencing wholly pleasant *vedanā*s.
mn12:42.4: Suppose, Sāriputta, there were a pond with clear water, sweet water, cool water, clean banks, delightful.
mn12:42.5: And not far from it there would be a thick woodland grove.
mn12:42.6: Then a man might come, scorched by the heat, oppressed by the heat, weary, thirsty, parched, heading along a single road straight toward that very pond.
mn12:42.7: A man with good eyesight, seeing him, might say:
mn12:42.8: ‘This worthy man is practicing in such a way and conducting himself in such a way, and has undertaken this path, that he will come to this very pond.’
mn12:42.9: Then later he might see him plunge into that pond, bathe and drink, and after calming all weariness, fatigue, and fever, come out and sit or lie down in that woodland grove, experiencing wholly pleasant *vedanā*s.
mn12:43.1: Whoever, Sāriputta, would say of me, knowing and seeing thus:
mn12:43.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama has no higher-than-human state, no distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones;
mn12:43.3: the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* hammered out by reasoning, following inquiry, of his own inspiration,’
mn12:43.4: then, Sāriputta, unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:43.5: Just as, Sāriputta, a *bhikkhu* accomplished in ethical conduct, accomplished in *samādhi*, accomplished in discernment [*paññā*], could realize final knowledge in this very life, so certain is this state of affairs, Sāriputta, I say.
mn12:43.6: Unless he abandons that speech, abandons that mind [*cittaṁ*], and relinquishes that view, he is cast down in hell as though carried there.
mn12:44.1: I recall, Sāriputta, having lived a holy life endowed with four factors:
mn12:44.2: I was austere, exceedingly austere; rough, exceedingly rough; scrupulous, exceedingly scrupulous; secluded, exceedingly secluded.
mn12:45.1: And here, Sāriputta, this was my austerity: I went naked, rejecting conventions, licking my hands, not coming when asked, not stopping when asked; I did not accept food brought to me, food made especially for me, or an invitation.
mn12:45.10: I was one who squatted, devoted to exertion in the squatting posture.
mn12:45.11: I was one who used a bed of thorns, making my bed on a thorny couch.
mn12:45.12: I lived devoted to the practice of entering water three times a day, including the evening.
mn12:45.13: Thus I lived devoted to many kinds of tormenting and scorching the body.
mn12:45.2: I accepted nothing from the mouth of a pot, nothing from the mouth of a bowl, nothing from across a threshold, across a stick, or across a pestle; nothing from two people eating together, from a pregnant woman, from a nursing woman, from a woman in the company of a man; not from where food had been announced, not where a dog stood by, not where flies swarmed. I ate no fish or meat, and drank no liquor, wine, or fermented brew.
mn12:45.3: I went to one house only and took one morsel; or to two houses and took two morsels … or to seven houses and took seven morsels.
mn12:45.4: I sustained myself on one serving, or on two servings … or on seven servings.
mn12:45.5: I ate once a day, or once every two days … or once every seven days. Thus I lived devoted to the practice of taking food at intervals of up to half a month.
mn12:45.6: I was an eater of greens, or of millet, or of wild rice, or of hide-parings, or of moss, or of rice bran, or of rice scum, or of oil-cake, or of grass, or of cow-dung. I lived on forest roots and fruits, feeding on fallen fruit.
mn12:45.7: I wore hemp cloth, mixed hemp cloth, shrouds, refuse rags, bark garments, antelope hide, strips of antelope hide, kusa-grass garments, bark-fiber garments, wood-shaving garments, human-hair blankets, horse-hair blankets, or owl-wing garments.
mn12:45.8: I was one who plucked out hair and beard, devoted to the practice of plucking out hair and beard.
mn12:45.9: I was one who stood continually, rejecting seats.
mn12:46.1: And here, Sāriputta, this was my roughness:
mn12:46.2: dust and dirt accumulated on my body for many years, building up in flakes.
mn12:46.3: Just as on the stump of a tinduka tree dust and dirt accumulate for many years, building up in flakes, so too on my body dust and dirt accumulated for many years, building up in flakes.
mn12:46.4: Yet the thought did not occur to me:
mn12:46.5: ‘Oh, if only I might rub off this dust and dirt with my hand, or others might rub off this dust and dirt from me with their hands.’
mn12:46.6: That did not occur to me either.
mn12:47.1: And here, Sāriputta, this was my scrupulousness:
mn12:47.2: I would step forward mindfully, I would step back mindfully, so much so that even for a drop of water compassion was present in me:
mn12:47.3: ‘Let me not bring harm to tiny creatures dwelling in uneven places.’
mn12:48.1: And here, Sāriputta, this was my seclusion [*pavivittasmiṁ*]:
mn12:48.2: I lived having plunged into a certain forest domain.
mn12:48.3: Whenever I saw a cowherd, a shepherd, a grass-cutter, a woodcutter, or a forest worker, I would flee from forest to forest, from thicket to thicket, from hollow to hollow, from high ground to high ground.
mn12:48.5: So that they might not see me, and I might not see them.
mn12:48.6: Just as a forest deer, on seeing humans, flees from forest to forest, from thicket to thicket, from hollow to hollow, from high ground to high ground,
mn12:48.7: so too, whenever I saw a cowherd, a shepherd, a grass-cutter, a woodcutter, or a forest worker, I would flee from forest to forest, from thicket to thicket, from hollow to hollow, from high ground to high ground.
mn12:48.9: So that they might not see me, and I might not see them.
mn12:49.1: Then, Sāriputta, I would go to cattle-pens after the cattle had been penned up and the cowherds had gone away, and there on all fours I would eat the dung of young suckling calves.
mn12:49.2: And so long as my own urine and excrement lasted, I would feed on my own urine and excrement.
mn12:49.3: This was my practice regarding very loathsome food, Sāriputta.
mn12:5.1: And yet even this inference in accordance with *dhamma* would not occur to Sunakkhatta, that foolish man, regarding me:
mn12:5.2: ‘The *Bhagavā* is an *arahant*, perfectly awakened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, one who has gone well, knower of the world, unsurpassed guide of persons to be trained, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.’
mn12:50.1: Then, Sāriputta, I lived having plunged into a certain terrifying stretch of woodland.
mn12:50.10: the sage intent upon the quest.’
mn12:50.2: And such was the terror of that terrifying woodland stretch, Sāriputta, that
mn12:50.3: whoever entered that woodland stretch while not free from lust, for the most part their hairs stood on end.
mn12:50.4: Then, Sāriputta, on those nights of winter, the cold season, the interval of eight days, the time of snowfall, on such nights I stayed in the open by night and in the woodland by day;
mn12:50.5: but in the last month of the hot season I stayed in the open by day and in the woodland by night.
mn12:50.6: And this astonishing verse, not heard before, occurred to me then, Sāriputta:
mn12:50.9: naked, not seated by a fire,
mn12:51.1: Then, Sāriputta, I made my bed in a charnel ground, using the bones of corpses as a pillow.
mn12:51.2: And village boys would come up to me and spit on me, urinate on me, throw dust on me, and stick splinters into my ears.
mn12:51.3: Yet I do not recall ever having aroused an evil mind [*cittaṁ*] toward them.
mn12:51.4: This was my dwelling in equanimity [*upekkhā*], Sāriputta.
mn12:52.1: There are, Sāriputta, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view:
mn12:52.10: But it should not be seen in that way, Sāriputta.
mn12:52.11: Even then a jujube was no larger than it is now.
mn12:52.12: From my taking just one jujube as food, my body reached the utmost emaciation.
mn12:52.13: Because of eating so little, my limbs became like the jointed segments of a vine.
mn12:52.14: Because of eating so little, my buttocks became like a camel’s hoofprint.
mn12:52.15: Because of eating so little, my spine stood out like a string of beads.
mn12:52.16: Because of eating so little, my ribs jutted out like the broken rafters of an old shed.
mn12:52.17: Because of eating so little, my eyeballs appeared deep in their sockets, like reflections of water deep in a well.
mn12:52.18: Because of eating so little, the skin on my head shriveled and withered, like a bitter gourd cut while still green shrivels and withers in wind and sun.
mn12:52.19: Then, Sāriputta, when I thought, ‘I will touch the skin of my belly,’ I took hold of my backbone itself; and when I thought, ‘I will touch my backbone,’ I took hold of the skin of my belly itself. So closely was the skin of my belly clinging to my backbone because of eating so little.
mn12:52.20: Then, Sāriputta, when I thought, ‘I will relieve myself or urinate,’ I fell over there and then on my face because of eating so little.
mn12:52.21: Then, Sāriputta, to soothe this very body, I rubbed my limbs with my hand. And while I rubbed my limbs with my hand, the hair, rotted at the roots, fell from my body because of eating so little.
mn12:52.5: They eat jujubes, they eat jujube powder, they drink jujube water—
mn12:52.6: they consume many kinds of jujube preparations.
mn12:52.7: But I recall, Sāriputta, having taken just one jujube as food.
mn12:52.9: ‘Surely at that time a jujube must have been large.’
mn12:53-55.1: There are, Sāriputta, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view:
mn12:53-55.11: ‘Surely at that time a grain of rice must have been large.’
mn12:53-55.12: But it should not be seen in that way, Sāriputta.
mn12:53-55.13: Even then a grain of rice was no larger than it is now.
mn12:53-55.14: From my taking just one grain of rice as food, my body reached the utmost emaciation.
mn12:53-55.15: Because of eating so little, my limbs became like the jointed segments of a vine.
mn12:53-55.16: Because of eating so little, my buttocks became like a camel’s hoofprint.
mn12:53-55.17: Because of eating so little, my spine stood out like a string of beads.
mn12:53-55.18: Because of eating so little, my ribs jutted out like the broken rafters of an old shed.
mn12:53-55.19: Because of eating so little, my eyeballs appeared deep in their sockets, like reflections of water deep in a well.
mn12:53-55.20: Because of eating so little, the skin on my head shriveled and withered, like a bitter gourd cut while still green shrivels and withers in wind and sun.
mn12:53-55.21: Then, Sāriputta, when I thought, ‘I will touch the skin of my belly,’ I took hold of my backbone itself; and when I thought, ‘I will touch my backbone,’ I took hold of the skin of my belly itself. So closely was the skin of my belly clinging to my backbone because of eating so little.
mn12:53-55.22: Then, Sāriputta, when I thought, ‘I will relieve myself or urinate,’ I fell over there and then on my face because of eating so little.
mn12:53-55.23: Then, Sāriputta, to soothe this very body, I rubbed my limbs with my hand. And while I rubbed my limbs with my hand, the hair, rotted at the roots, fell from my body because of eating so little.
mn12:53-55.7: They eat rice, they eat rice powder, they drink rice water—
mn12:53-55.8: they consume many kinds of rice preparations.
mn12:53-55.9: But I recall, Sāriputta, having taken just one grain of rice as food.
mn12:56.1: Yet, Sāriputta, by that way of life, by that practice, by that painful striving, I did not attain any higher-than-human state, any distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.
mn12:56.3: Because I did not attain that noble discernment [*paññā*] which, when attained, is noble, leading onward, and leads one who acts in accordance with it to the complete ending of *dukkha*.
mn12:57.1: There are, Sāriputta, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view:
mn12:57.3: But that *saṃsāra*, Sāriputta, is not easy to find such that I have not already wandered through it in this long course of time, except among the gods of the Pure Abodes.
mn12:57.4: And if I were to wander among the gods of the Pure Abodes, Sāriputta, I would not return again to this world.
mn12:58.1: There are, Sāriputta, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view:
mn12:58.3: But that rebirth, Sāriputta, is not easy to find such that I have not already been reborn there in this long course of time, except among the gods of the Pure Abodes.
mn12:58.4: And if I were to be reborn among the gods of the Pure Abodes, Sāriputta, I would not return again to this world.
mn12:59.1: There are, Sāriputta, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view:
mn12:59.3: But that abode, Sāriputta, is not easy to find such that I have not already dwelt there in this long course of time, except among the gods of the Pure Abodes.
mn12:59.4: And if I were to dwell among the gods of the Pure Abodes, Sāriputta, I would not return again to this world.
mn12:6.1: And yet even this inference in accordance with *dhamma* would not occur to Sunakkhatta, that foolish man, regarding me:
mn12:6.2: ‘The *Bhagavā* experiences manifold kinds of psychic power: having been one, he becomes many; having been many, he becomes one. He appears and disappears. He goes unhindered through walls, ramparts, and mountains as though through space. He dives in and out of the earth as though in water. He walks on water without breaking through, as though on earth. Seated cross-legged, he travels through the sky like a winged bird. With his hand he touches and strokes even the moon and sun, so mighty and powerful. He exercises mastery with the body as far as the Brahmā world.’
mn12:60.1: There are, Sāriputta, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view:
mn12:60.3: But that sacrifice, Sāriputta, is not easy to find that has not already been offered by me in this long course of time, and that as a wealthy anointed noble king or as a wealthy brahmin.
mn12:61.1: There are, Sāriputta, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view:
mn12:61.3: But that fire, Sāriputta, is not easy to find that has not already been tended by me in this long course of time, and that as a wealthy anointed noble king or as a wealthy brahmin.
mn12:62.1: There are, Sāriputta, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view:
mn12:62.10: Except for the time spent in eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; except for attending to urination and defecation; except for dispelling drowsiness and fatigue—the Tathāgata’s teaching of *dhamma* would still not be exhausted, the Tathāgata’s wording and phrasing of *dhamma* would still not be exhausted, the Tathāgata’s ready answer to questions would still not be exhausted.
mn12:62.11: Then those four disciples, each a hundred years old and living a hundred years, might pass away when a hundred years had elapsed.
mn12:62.12: Even if you were to carry me about on a bed, Sāriputta, there would be no change in the acuteness of discernment of the Tathāgata.
mn12:62.2: ‘So long as this worthy man is young, youthful, black-haired, endowed with the blessing of youth, in the prime of life, just so long is he endowed with the highest acuteness of discernment.
mn12:62.3: But when this worthy man becomes old, aged, elderly, advanced in years, having reached the final stage of life—eighty or ninety or a hundred years old by birth—then that acuteness of discernment declines.’
mn12:62.4: But it should not be seen in that way, Sāriputta.
mn12:62.5: For now, Sāriputta, I am old, aged, elderly, advanced in years, having reached the final stage of life; I am now eighty years old.
mn12:62.6: Suppose I had four disciples, Sāriputta, each a hundred years old, living a hundred years, endowed with supreme *sati*, speed, steadiness, and the highest acuteness of discernment.
mn12:62.7: Just as a strong archer, trained, practiced, skilled, might easily shoot a light arrow across the shadow of a palmyra tree,
mn12:62.8: so too they would be thus supremely mindful, thus possessed of supreme speed, thus possessed of supreme steadiness, and thus endowed with the highest acuteness of discernment.
mn12:62.9: They might ask me question after question concerning the four *satipaṭṭhānas*; and as each question was asked, I might answer it, and they might retain my answer exactly as answered, yet they would not put a second question beyond that.
mn12:63.1: If anyone, Sāriputta, rightly speaking were to say:
mn12:63.2: ‘A being of non-deluded nature has arisen in the world for the welfare of the many, for the *sukha* of the many, out of compassion for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and *sukha* of gods and humans,’ he would rightly say that of me:
mn12:63.3: ‘A being of non-deluded nature has arisen in the world for the welfare of the many, for the *sukha* of the many, out of compassion for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and *sukha* of gods and humans.’”
mn12:64.1: Now at that time Venerable Nāgasamāla was standing behind the *Bhagavā*, fanning the *Bhagavā*.
mn12:64.2: Then Venerable Nāgasamāla said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn12:64.4: For, *bhante*, on hearing this exposition of *dhamma*, the hairs of my body stand on end.
mn12:64.5: What is the name of this exposition of *dhamma*, *bhante*?”
mn12:64.6: “Then in that case, Nāgasamāla, remember this exposition of *dhamma* as ‘The Hair-Raising Exposition.’”
mn12:64.8: Satisfied, Venerable Nāgasamāla rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* said.
mn12:64.9: The second, The Greater Lion’s Roar, is finished.
mn12:7.1: And yet even this inference in accordance with *dhamma* would not occur to Sunakkhatta, that foolish man, regarding me:
mn12:7.2: ‘The *Bhagavā*, with the divine element of hearing, purified and surpassing the human, hears both kinds of sounds, divine and human, those far and near.’
mn12:8.1: And yet even this inference in accordance with *dhamma* would not occur to Sunakkhatta, that foolish man, regarding me:
mn12:8.10: and a scattered mind [*cittaṁ*] as a scattered mind [*cittaṁ*];
mn12:8.11: he understands an expanded mind [*cittaṁ*] as an expanded mind [*cittaṁ*],
mn12:8.12: and an unexpanded mind [*cittaṁ*] as an unexpanded mind [*cittaṁ*];
mn12:8.13: he understands a surpassable mind [*cittaṁ*] as a surpassable mind [*cittaṁ*],
mn12:8.14: and an unsurpassable mind [*cittaṁ*] as an unsurpassable mind [*cittaṁ*];
mn12:8.15: he understands a mind [*cittaṁ*] endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*] as a mind [*cittaṁ*] endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*],
mn12:8.16: and a mind [*cittaṁ*] not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ*] as a mind [*cittaṁ*] not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ*];
mn12:8.17: he understands a liberated mind [*cittaṁ*] as a liberated mind [*cittaṁ*],
mn12:8.18: and an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*] as an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*].’
mn12:8.2: ‘The *Bhagavā* understands the mind [*ceto*] of other beings, other persons, having encompassed it with his mind [*cetasā*]:
mn12:8.3: he understands a mind [*cittaṁ*] with lust as a mind [*cittaṁ*] with lust,
mn12:8.4: and a mind [*cittaṁ*] without lust as a mind [*cittaṁ*] without lust;
mn12:8.5: he understands a mind [*cittaṁ*] with hatred as a mind [*cittaṁ*] with hatred,
mn12:8.6: and a mind [*cittaṁ*] without hatred as a mind [*cittaṁ*] without hatred;
mn12:8.7: he understands a mind [*cittaṁ*] with delusion as a mind [*cittaṁ*] with delusion,
mn12:8.8: and a mind [*cittaṁ*] without delusion as a mind [*cittaṁ*] without delusion;
mn12:8.9: he understands a contracted mind [*cittaṁ*] as a contracted mind [*cittaṁ*],
mn12:9.1: These are the ten Tathāgata *balas*, Sāriputta, by which the Tathāgata, endowed with those *balas*, claims the leader’s place, roars the lion’s roar in the assemblies, and sets rolling the holy wheel.
